Most likely, but not guaranteed, you're paying attention. The listener has the advantage. If the listener is tactically listening, because the talker is revealing information and doing all the work, therefore wearing themselves down. As they wear themselves down, they are more likely to pose their communication to their own team using the phraseology and ideas you have suggested. The listener has the opportunity to harness the talker's energy and guide them and their team through the process to the listener's end. This is Listener's Judo and why some people describe negotiation as the art of letting someone have your way. Great negotiation isn't about strong arming your opponent into submission. Smart, strategic, tactical negotiation is about using your counterparts' words and energy to get to your objective. A tool we've developed here at the Black Swan Group specifically for this is the calibrated questions. These are the refined subset of what the FBI and many others call open-ended questions. Interrogative questions or the reporter's questions. These begin with the who, what, when, where, why, and how, but we'll focus them down and strategically combine them for deliberate effects. We want to specifically employ these questions in a way designed or calibrated to remove aggression, gain information, focus attention, be the least threatening, and trigger the most in-depth stop-and-think behavior. Talk about a bonanza. These give our counterpart the feeling of control, of being vested in the process, and of simultaneously wearing them out. These also allow you to invisibly introduce ideas and requests without sounding like you're demanding something or creating obstacles. Simply sequenced, your problems become their problems, and they feel the need to find a solution for you in order to maintain control. I'll go over calibrated questions more in-depth in future chapters, but for now, I just want to show you a quick example of how easy and simple this could be. If you examine the statement, you can't leave. You'll see that it is quite pushy and even somewhat hostile, but if you rephrase it as, what do you hope to achieve by leaving? You remove your overt aggression, and suddenly, they're more likely to stop and think and open up. If you're going into a negotiation with the mentality that you need to grapple, argue, or logic your counterpart into submission, then you're never going to achieve your desired outcomes in a lasting fashion. Brow beating or coercing the other side will almost never work in your favor. Perceived victories are fleeting. Please learn to see your counterpart as more than just some obstacle to get what you want. There are also a person with needs, emotions, and desires who's confronting the same problem in the environment that you are. Otherwise, you wouldn't be seeking a deal. Rather than overpowering them, you need to entice them. And rather than conquering them, you need to co-op them. A master negotiator gets their counterpart to do the work for them and come up with the solutions themselves. When it's a solution they've come up with, they will fight harder for it within their team to make it happen. If you can't reach beyond your counterpart, then all you've got with their implementers is hope, and that isn't a strategy. However, if you can get your counterpart to work for you, they will be able to reach the other decision makers, implementers, and deal breakers to solve your problems for you. You are giving them the illusion of control while you are actually guiding the process.
